items.question: What is the average time complexity of looking up a value by key in a Dictionary<TKey,TValue>?
items.explanation: Hashing the key computes the bucket directly — no scanning. Worst case degrades to O(n) only with mass collisions.

items.question: Why is accessing arr[i] in an array O(1)?
options: Arrays are cached by the OS
The address is computed directly: base + i × elementSize
The runtime keeps an index of positions
items.explanation: Contiguous memory means simple address arithmetic — no traversal needed.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Arrays: Memory Layout and Why Cache Matters

items.question: Which data structure processes items strictly first-in, first-out?
items.explanation: Queue = FIFO (Enqueue at the back, Dequeue from the front). Stack is the opposite (LIFO).

items.question: Building a string with += inside a loop of n iterations costs overall:
options: O(n²) — each concatenation copies the whole string so far
items.explanation: .NET strings are immutable; every += allocates and copies. Use StringBuilder for loops.

items.question: What must be true about the data before you can apply binary search?
items.explanation: Binary search discards half the range each step — only valid when order tells you which half to keep.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Binary Search: Beyond Finding an Element

items.question: A balanced binary tree contains 1,000,000 nodes. Its height is approximately:
items.explanation: Height ≈ log₂(n) ≈ 20 — the reason balanced trees (and database indexes) are fast.

items.question: The cleanest way to remove duplicates from a list of emails is:
items.explanation: HashSet ignores duplicates on Add in O(1) each — one pass, done. Sorting works but is O(n log n) and changes order.

items.question: Code calls orders.FirstOrDefault(o => o.Id == id) inside a loop over 10,000 customers. The right fix is:
options: Build a Dictionary<int,Order> once before the loop
items.explanation: O(n×m) becomes O(n+m): one dictionary build converts each in-loop lookup to O(1). The most common real-world optimization.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Choosing the Right Data Structure: A Decision Framework

items.question: "Find the longest substring without repeating characters" is a classic fit for which technique?
items.explanation: Contiguous + optimize = sliding window: expand right, shrink left on violation — O(n).
items.relatedLessonTitle: Sliding Window: Substrings and Subarrays in O(n)

items.question: Why does BFS find shortest paths in an unweighted graph?
options: It explores all nodes at distance k before any node at distance k+1
items.explanation: The queue processes nodes in distance order — first arrival at a node used the fewest edges.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Graph BFS: Shortest Paths and Degrees of Separation

items.question: LINQ's OrderBy(...).ThenBy(...) works correctly because OrderBy is:
options: Stable — equal keys keep their relative order
items.explanation: ThenBy refines groups of equal primary keys — only meaningful if the primary sort preserved their order. Array.Sort is NOT stable.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Sorting I: What O(n log n) Buys You

items.question: Two-pointer technique on a sorted array finds a pair summing to a target in O(n) because:
options: Each comparison eliminates one candidate from consideration permanently
items.explanation: Sum too small → left++ (need bigger); too big → right-- (need smaller). Every step discards one element for good.

items.question: To find the 10 largest values in a stream of 10 million numbers with minimal memory, use:
options: A min-heap of size 10 — replace the minimum when something bigger arrives
A sorted list updated per item
items.explanation: O(n log 10) time, O(10) memory. The heap's min is the weakest of the current top-10.

items.question: Naive QuickSort degrades to O(n²) when:
options: Partitions are consistently maximally unbalanced (e.g., sorted input with first-element pivot)
n is a power of two
items.explanation: Every partition splitting n into 1 and n-1 gives n levels of O(n) work. Random/median pivots avoid it.

items.question: Kahn's topological sort finishes with fewer nodes in its output than the graph contains. This proves:
items.explanation: Nodes in a cycle never reach in-degree 0, so they're never emitted — free cycle detection.

items.question: Dijkstra's algorithm fails with negative edge weights because:
options: A settled node's distance can't be revisited, but a negative edge could later provide a cheaper path
It only fails with negative cycles
items.explanation: The greedy proof assumes settled = final; negative edges break that assumption. Bellman-Ford handles them.

items.question: Memoization turns naive recursive Fibonacci from O(2ⁿ) to O(n) because:
options: There are only n distinct subproblems; caching bounds work at one computation each
items.explanation: The exponential call tree collapses: repeated subproblems become O(1) lookups.

items.question: In 0/1 knapsack, dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-wᵢ] + vᵢ). The two arms represent:
options: Skipping item i vs taking it (paying weight, gaining value)
items.explanation: Each item has exactly two futures — excluded or included — and the recurrence takes the better one.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Dynamic Programming II: Tabulation and Classic Problems

items.question: Path compression speeds up Union-Find by:
options: Re-pointing every node visited during Find directly at the root
items.explanation: After one Find, the whole path hangs off the root — subsequent Finds on those nodes are O(1). With union-by-rank: near-constant amortized.

items.question: In backtracking, effective pruning matters because:
options: The search space is exponential — rejecting doomed branches early is the difference between milliseconds and impossible
It guarantees the first solution is optimal
items.explanation: Worst case stays exponential; pruning cuts the actual explored tree by orders of magnitude.
